---
title: "When feature importance points to the wrong lever"
subtitle: "A reproducible companion to the Causal SHAP paper"
toc: false
---

Machine-learning explanations are supposed to tell you *what to change*. But the
most popular one — SHAP — answers a different question: **what helps the model
predict?** On a causal graph those two questions come apart. Features next to the
outcome win credit they did not earn, while the upstream causes you could
actually intervene on fade into the background.

We show this with pretend DAGs: simulate data from a known causal structure,
then hide the structure and watch ordinary SHAP rebuild a distorted picture — a
homunculus with over-developed nodes near the outcome.

![Truth vs ordinary SHAP vs structural Causal SHAP on the toy DAG. Node area is attribution share; blue means under-credited, amber over-credited. Ordinary SHAP inflates the collider `ClinicVisit` — which has **zero** total effect — into the largest node.](assets/figures/toy_chain_fork_collider_homunculus.png)

::: {.pullquote}
On the teaching data, ordinary SHAP's importance ranking is *negatively*
correlated with the causal truth (Kendall τ ≈ −0.33). Structural Causal SHAP
flips it positive.
:::

## What the evidence actually says

| Evidence tier | Result | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Teaching DAGs** | Zero-effect proxies can dominate ordinary SHAP. | A controlled demonstration of proximity bias, not substantive NASA evidence. |
| **NASA flagship** | Ordinary and DAG-ordering SHAP are statistically tied. | Ordering features by a DAG is not enough to recover causal importance. |
| **Structural prototype** | Kendall τ rises to 0.794 and top-five recovery to 1.00. | Encouraging intervention propagation, pending scale-up and paired uncertainty. |

The project therefore does not claim that every method called “Causal SHAP”
wins. Its narrower claim is that the **coalition value function** must model how
an intervention propagates through descendants; ordering alone leaves the
predictive question largely unchanged.

## The workflow ladder

This site is organized as a ladder. Each rung is a move you make when a prediction
model is not enough and you need to reason about interventions.

![](assets/figures/ladder.svg){.ladder-hero}

| Rung | Move | What it buys you |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | [Vanilla SHAP](workflow/00-vanilla-shap.qmd) | See the failure: proximity bias toward the outcome. |
| **1** | [Causal discovery](workflow/01-causal-discovery.qmd) | Propose structure from data — a hypothesis, not an oracle. |
| **2** | [Complexity score](workflow/02-complexity-score.qmd) | Judge how much causal care the problem demands. |
| **3** | [Causal SHAP](workflow/03-causal-shap.qmd) | Propagate `do(X = x)` and credit upstream causes. |
| **4** | [Validation](workflow/04-validation.qmd) | Stress-test estimators where every answer is known. |
| **5** | [Iteration](workflow/05-iteration.qmd) | Refine the DAG and re-validate; report uncertainty. |

## Explore it

- **[Run the interactive app](app.qmd)** — climb every rung on four datasets, and
  run discovery and validation live.
- **[Cheatsheets](cheatsheets/dag-vocabulary.qmd)** — printable quick references for
  DAG vocabulary, discovery algorithms, SHAP variants, and estimands.
- **[Reproduce everything](reproducibility.qmd)** — the two-language pipeline, seeds,
  and a frozen-output gate.

::: {.callout-note}
Every dataset here is **synthetic**. Graph topologies for the flagship NASA
renal-stone example are source-exact, but all coefficients are simulation
choices. See [About](about.qmd) for provenance.
:::
